coredata.dc:title: Dual Z-scheme heterojunction Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 for increased photocatalytic degradation of the food additive tartrazine, in the presence of persulfate: Kinetics, toxicity, and density functional theory studies

coredata.dc:description: This study involved the synthesis of a Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 composite through hydrothermal methods, followed by mechanical grinding. The resulting heterojunction exhibited improved catalytic activity under visible light by effectively separating electrons and holes (e−/h+). The degradation of Tartrazine (TTZ) reached 93.20% within 50 min by employing a ternary composite at a concentration of 10 mg L−1, along with 6 mg L−1 of PS. The highest pseudo-first-order kinetic constant (0.1273 min−1 and R2 = 0.951) was observed in this system. The dual Z-scheme heterojunction is developed by Ce2Sn2O7, Ag3PO4, and V@g-C3N4, and it may increase the visible light absorption range while also accelerating charge carrier transfer and separation between catalysts. The analysis of the vulnerability positions and degradation pathways of TTZ involved the utilization of density functional theory (DFT) and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) to examine the intermediate products. Therefore, Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 is an excellent ternary nanocomposite for the remediation of pollutants.

head.ce:title: Dual Z-scheme heterojunction Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 for increased photocatalytic degradation of the food additive tartrazine, in the presence of persulfate: Kinetics, toxicity, and density functional theory studies

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study involved the synthesis of a Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 composite through hydrothermal methods, followed by mechanical grinding. The resulting heterojunction exhibited improved catalytic activity under visible light by effectively separating electrons and holes (e−/h+). The degradation of Tartrazine (TTZ) reached 93.20% within 50 min by employing a ternary composite at a concentration of 10 mg L−1, along with 6 mg L−1 of PS. The highest pseudo-first-order kinetic constant (0.1273 min−1 and R2 = 0.951) was observed in this system. The dual Z-scheme heterojunction is developed by Ce2Sn2O7, Ag3PO4, and V@g-C3N4, and it may increase the visible light absorption range while also accelerating charge carrier transfer and separation between catalysts. The analysis of the vulnerability positions and degradation pathways of TTZ involved the utilization of density functional theory (DFT) and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) to examine the intermediate products. Therefore, Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 is an excellent ternary nanocomposite for the remediation of pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The presence of organic contaminants such as azo dyes in water is a serious issue because of their harmful effects on water quality. These contaminants are primarily discharged through effluents from food, textile, paper, cosmetic, pharmaceutical, and printing sectors (Narasaiah and Mandal, 2020). Tartrazine (TTZ) is a yellow dye commonly found in drinks, bakery items, and sweeteners (Xu et al., 2015). It is linked to adverse effects, such as hyperactivity, asthma, migraines, thyroid cancer, and lupus (Dotto et al., 2012). Therefore, the development of efficient and long-term strategies for removing tartrazine from wastewater is crucial. Traditional methods of removing organic contaminants from water include precipitation, ion exchange, and adsorption (Ratshiedana et al., 2021; Skinner et al., 2020). However, many of these approaches are limited by cost, secondary contaminants, high energy consumption, and reduced removal efficacy (Dhaliwal et al., 2020). Consequently, alternative strategies that avoid these issues are currently being used. One such strategy, which involves the use of sulfate radical (SO4 •−) aided advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), is gaining popularity for degrading hazardous, refractory, and carcinogenic water contaminants by employing SO4 •− as a highly oxidizing species (Li et al., 2022). The production of SO4 •− occurs when persulfate (PS) or peroxymonosulfate (PMS) is activated through processes like irradiation, heat, the presence of transition metal ions, metal oxides, carbonaceous materials, and other similar methods (Ntelane et al., 2023). SO4 •− is expected to exhibit greater stability across a larger pH range (2.0–10), increased selectivity, and a higher redox potential (E = 2.5–3.1 V) compared to hydroxyl radicals (•OH) (E = 2.8 V) (Guerra-Rodríguez et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2022; Wang and Wang, 2018). Mixing carbonaceous materials with a PS system can result in clear synergistic advantages in the degradation process. The carbon material, serving as an electron transmitter/activator, can efficiently activate PS and create more sulfate free radicals on the surface of the carbon particles, thereby facilitating the degradation of organic pollutant (Shi et al., 2022).

Furthermore, the combination of multiple metal oxides (MOs) and carbon materials in semiconductors leads to the formation of either homojunction or heterojunction interfaces. These interfaces enhance the electron migration capability and lifespan of photo-generated species (Tahir et al., 2022). The generation of defects in the structure of the photocatalyst can also induce changes in the electronic structure. These nanocomposite photocatalysts exhibit a range of advantageous characteristics, including a low band gap, prolonged lifespan of photogenerated species, enhanced active sites, and exceptional stability within a unified design (Janani et al., 2021). In a recent study, Ibrahim et al. successfully synthesized a ternary nanocomposite comprising of WO3/CuO/rGO. This nanocomposite has demonstrated remarkable capabilities in improving the photocatalytic degradation of multiple organic pollutants, while also exhibiting antimicrobial activity (Alsafari et al., 2023). Iftekhar et al. developed double Z-scheme CdS@g-C3N4/Bi2MoO6 ternary nanocomposite. This nanocomposite exhibits excellent performance in various applications such as photocatalytic degradation in visible light, adsorption, and electrochemical processes (Ahmad et al., 2023).

In recent years, there has been a surge of interest in the applications of graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) including optoelectronics, sensors, solar cells, water splitting, environmental pollutant reduction, photocatalytic decomposition of organic pollutants, photocatalytic generation of H2, and oxygen reduction and evolution reactions (Liu et al., 2017; Singh et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2016). However, pure g-C3N4 faces several constraints, including a notable recombination rate of electron-hole pairs produced by light and a restricted ability for charge transfer (Ma et al., 2017; Vattikuti et al., 2020). The disadvantages of g-C3N4, many researchers have pursued various strategies such as changing the bandgap using heteroatom doping, constructing heterojunctions, and designing the morphology (Fu et al., 2018; Jiang et al., 2018; Ma et al., 2016). A wide range of approaches have been utilized to augment the photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4. These include impregnating dopants, generating a porous structure, and integrating supplementary semiconductors into the g-C3N4 structure to form heterojunctions (Ma et al., 2012). Boron doping has been used to boost the reactivity of carbon-based materials such as g-C3N4 (Bora and Mewada, 2017), along with other dopants such as phosphorus (Chai et al., 2017) For the same reason, vanadium (V) is included in the g-C3N4 matrix. However, research on V@g-C3N4 for photocatalytic applications is limited.

Photocatalysis is an attractive technique for environmental remediation. However, it has been well explored in the past, revealing certain drawbacks. Semiconductor photocatalysis technology has garnered significant attention in research circles due to its exceptional photocatalytic efficiency, affordability, and user-friendly nature for eliminating organic pollutants (Suebsom et al., 2020). Ceramic pyrochlore oxides are frequently represented as A2B2O7, where A can be Er, Tb, Eu, Ce, Gd, or Sm, and B can be Ti, Sn, Zn, or Zr. The stability and crystal structure of pyrochlores are significantly influenced by the interplay between the radii of the A-site cation (rA3+) and B-site cation (rB4+), along with the presence of oxygen vacancies. Pyrochlore oxides, including Y2Ti2O7, Eu2Ti2O7, Y2Ru2O7, and Nd2Sn2O7, have been synthesized via the amalgamation of crystal structures (Feng et al., 2020; Munawar et al., 2019; C. C. Sun et al., 2021; Zinatloo-Ajabshir et al., 2020b). Rare-earth or lanthanide stannates have become increasingly popular as catalysts in various processes such as photocatalysis, electrochemical conversion, and energy storage. This is primarily due to their remarkable properties, which make them highly effective in these applications (Kaliyaperumal et al., 2020; Zinatloo-Ajabshir et al., 2020a). Cerium stannate (Ce2Sn2O7) is a pyrochlore-type oxide with semiconducting properties, which has found wide application in catalytic research. Jayaraman et al. developed a CdZnS solid-solution-supported Ce2Sn2O7 pyrochlore photocatalyst; this pyrochlore photocatalyst showed better photocatalytic activity than single components such as CdZnS, Ce2Sn2O7, and pure CdS, demonstrating its effectiveness as a substitute for organic pollution removal (Jayaraman et al., 2019a) Talebzadeh et al. have successful integration of erbium stannate nanoparticles into carbon nanocomposites has yielded remarkable improvements in the material's photocatalytic capabilities (Talebzadeh et al., 2022). These results suggest that Ce2Sn2O7 can be used as a photocatalyst for wastewater treatment.

The field of visible-light-responsive semiconductor photocatalysts has witnessed a significant leap forward with the remarkable discovery of Ag3PO4, which exhibits substantial absorption of visible light below 520 nm (Bi et al., 2011; He et al., 2016). Ag3PO4 absorbs visible light and has a body-centered cubic structure with a small band gap of 2.36–2.43 eV (Ghazalian et al., 2017) Several morphologies of Ag3PO4 have were created over time, including tetrapods, tetrahedrons, cubes, spheres, and tris octahedra (Bi et al., 2012; J.F. Cruz-Filho et al., 2019; Dong et al., 2014; Khan et al., 2012) Although research has revealed that Ag3PO4 has a great photocatalytic efficacy, it also has a high recombination rate for photogenerated charge carriers. Fan et al. developed a ZnFe2O4/Ag3PO4/g-C3N4 photocatalyst with outstanding activity under light and demonstrated excellent potential for practical use in the treatment of HABs (Fan et al., 2022). Wang et al. prepared a new Z-scheme Sr2MgSi2O7:Eu2+, Dy3+/Ag3PO4 photocatalyst for the removal of pollutants and hydrogen generation (Wang et al., 2022) The development of novel ternary photocatalysts provides an effective and ecologically favorable technique for eliminating organic contaminants during wastewater treatment.

The objective of this study was to evaluate the efficiency of Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4/Vis/PS as a photocatalyst by examining its ability to degrade TTZ. The examination of various operational factors in photocatalysis involved analyzing the initial TTZ concentration, catalyst dosage, PS concentration, and pH levels of the reaction mixtures. Furthermore, the reusability and stability of Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 were evaluated through a series of four consecutive cycles. The degradation pathways of TTZ were investigated through the utilization of kinetics and DFT calculations. The band structures of Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4/Vis/PS are determined by proposing and utilizing a plausible photocatalytic reaction mechanism. The outcomes of this study possess the capability to make a substantial contribution to the advancement of PS-mediated dual-Z-scheme catalysts. These catalysts have the potential to efficiently eradicate harmful substances in wastewater treatment applications, thereby aiding in the preservation of the environment.
The supplementary information (Sections S1, S2, and S3) contains comprehensive details about the chemicals used, the synthetic procedure employed, and the characterization of the photocatalyst.

The degradation performance of the ternary nanocomposite Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 was investigated by placing a TTZ solution (50 mL; 20 mg L−1) in a beaker. Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 (10 mg L−1) was added to a 50 mL beaker containing the TTZ solution. Adding PS (6 mg L−1) to the TTZ solution, the suspension was subjected to irradiation utilizing a 60 W halogen projector lamp as the light source. During the irradiation process, samples of the suspension (3 mL) were taken at specific time intervals (10 min). The Ultraviolet–visible spectrometer was used to measure the concentration of TTZ after centrifuging the samples at 4000 rpm.

The determination of the maximum TTZ uptake (qe) was conducted by employing Eq. (1).

The initial and final concentration of TTZ is denoted as Co and Ce (mg L−1) respectively. The volume of TTZ is represented by v (mL) and the weight dosage of Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 is denoted as m (mg).

The XRD patterns presented in Fig. S1a were obtained to assess the phase purity and crystal behavior of bulk g-C3N4, V@g-C3N4, Ce2Sn2O7, Ag3PO4, and Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 ternary nanocomposites. Fig. S1a illustrates that the patterns of V@g-C3N4 closely resemble those of g-C3N4, indicating a significant preservation of the original crystal structure in V@g-C3N4. The presence of (100) and (002) Miller indices in these samples can be mainly attributed to the periodic arrangement of layers in conjugated aromatic systems and the formation of nitrogen bonds within the tri-s-triazine motifs (Martin et al., 2014). The (100) and (002) peaks exhibit a slight shift towards lower angles. The (100) peak of V@g-C3N4 exhibited a noticeable shift from 12.88° to 12.38°, while the (002) peak experienced a shift from 27.87° to 27.61°, suggesting an expansion in the interlayer spacing when compared to bulk g-C3N4. The V atom that was doped showed a greater atomic radius in comparison to the substituted C atom, which could be attributed to the negative angle shift and the resulting expansion of the interlayer distance (Zhao et al., 2017). The absence of peaks corresponding to Ag and its oxides further confirms the purity of the synthesized Ag3PO4. The diffraction pattern analysis revealed the presence of specific crystallographic planes based on the observed 2θ values. The indexed planes were determined to be (110), (200), (210), (211), (310), (222), (320), (321), and (421) corresponding to 2θ values of 20.80°, 29.76°, 33.21°, 36.57°, 47.77°, 52.58°, 54.98°, 57.31°, and 71.96°, respectively. The synthesized pristine Ag3PO4 exhibited a body-centered cubic structure, with a space group of P43n (J. F. Cruz-Filho et al., 2019). The Ce2Sn2O7 diffraction peaks, which were located at 28.72°, 33.37°, 47.67°, 51.98°, 54.73°, 56.62°, 59.45°, 69.71°, and 77.03°, are consistent with the (222), (400), (440), (442), (620), (622), (444), (800), and (662) planes, respectively (Jayaraman et al., 2019b). The successful formation of the nanocomposite was achieved through the observed diffraction peaks of V@g-C3N4, Ce2Sn2O7, and Ag3PO4 in the ternary nanocomposite.

In Fig. S1b, the FT-IR spectra of bulk g-C3N4, V@g-C3N4, Ag3PO4, Ce2Sn2O7, and the ternary nanocomposite of Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 are presented. The stretching modes of the aromatic C–N heterocycles were detected in the spectral range of 1200–1700 cm−1 in both the bulk g-C3N4, V@g-C3N4 spectra. In addition, the heptazine units exhibited a breathing mode, which was measured at a frequency of 811.35 cm−1 (Erşan, 2016). The peak observed between 3000 and 3500 cm−1 cans be attributed to the stretching mode of hydroxyl groups (Martin et al., 2014). The stretching vibration of P–O bonds in Ag3PO4 is responsible for the absorption band observed at 945.69 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum. On the other hand, the bending vibration of O–P–O gives rise to the absorption band at 543.99 cm−1 (Li et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2017a). The vibration of OH in the adsorbed water is represented by a wide peak of low intensity, ranging from 3000 to 3500 cm−1 (Zhang et al., 2014). Two vibrational bands were observed at 490.47 and 674.59 cm−1 in the Ce2Sn2O7 sample. The Sn–O stretching vibrations are represented by the high-intensity band at 647.59 cm−1, while the low-intensity band at 1059.47 cm−1 corresponds to the O–Sn–O bending vibrations. The band detected at 490.47 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of the Ce–O bond within the pyrochlore structure (Balamurugan et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2017b). In the ternary nanocomposite, the spectral peaks originating from V@g-C3N4, Ce2Sn2O7, and Ag3PO4 were detected, signifying the distinct contributions of each component nanomaterial.

The morphological characteristics of the various photocatalysts were extensively examined using FE-SEM and TEM techniques. The FE-SEM images of V@g-C3N4, Ag3PO4, Ce2Sn2O7, and Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 nanocomposite is depicted in Fig. 2(a), (b), (c), and (d), respectively. V@g-C3N4 has a stacked sheet-like structure (Fig. 1 a). As shown in Fig. 1b, Ce2Sn2O7 has a sphere-like structure. The even distribution of the polyhedral structure of Ag3PO4 is depicted in Fig. 1c. The prepared ternary nanocomposite, as depicted in Fig. 1d, exhibits well-defined morphologies that can be readily identified.

The internal morphology of the Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 nanocomposite was analyzed using TEM. The TEM micrographs of the prepared ternary nanocomposite, as depicted in Fig. 1e and f, exhibit distinct nanocomposite morphologies of the constituent components, namely V@g-C3N4, Ce2Sn2O7, and Ag3PO4. The HR-TEM image of the ternary photocatalyst (Fig. 1g) exhibits clearly defined heterojunction boundaries among the three components. The crystal planes of Ce2Sn2O7 (222) and Ag3PO4 (210) were specifically identified, with d-spacings of 0.282 nm and 0.261 nm, respectively. The XRD findings confirm the SAED pattern of the Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 nanocomposite, as shown in Fig. 1h. Elemental mapping (Ce, Sn, Ag, P, V, C, N, and O) images of the Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 are shown in Fig. 1i–p. The Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 nanocomposite (Fig. 1q) was analyzed using EDS, which detected the presence of various elements such as C, N, O, P, Ag, Sn, Ce, and V. The mass fractions of these elements (inset of Fig. 1q) were found to be 16.59%, 10.76%, 9.07%, 0.51%, 16.65%, 24.58%, 18.96%, and 2.87%, respectively. Consequently, the morphological analysis confirms the progress made in the development of the ternary nanocomposite Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4.

XPS was used to evaluate the chemical states and elemental compositions of Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 nanocomposites. Fig. S2a displays the survey spectrum of the V@g-C3N4, Ce2Sn2O, Ag3PO4, and Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 nanocomposites. In addition, the samples' core-level spectra were examined. Fig. S2b demonstrated the core-level spectra of Ce 3d5/2 and Ce 3d3/2, which is suggested that the observed peaks were matched to eight minor peaks associated with oxidation states of Ce3+ and Ce4+, respectively. The peaks at 894.9, 878, 905.8, and 882.9 eV indicate the oxidation states of Ce3+. Furthermore, the peaks located at 914.6, 896.2, 905.07, and 880.4 eV indicate states of Ce 4+ (Jayaraman et al., 2019b; Qian et al., 2017). Fig. S2c displays a deconvoluted Sn spectrum. A spin–orbit splitting of 9.23 eV, indicates for the peaks at 493.32 and 484.09 eV owing to the Sn 3d3/2 and Sn 3d5/2, respectively, confirmed the Sn4+ (Ganesan et al., 2023). The XPS spectra of Ag 3d (Fig. S2d) of Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 has two peaks positioned at 366.4 and 372.3 eV, and these peaks can be attributed to Ag+ in the 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 states of Ag3PO4, respectively (You et al., 2018). Fig. S2g shows the deconvoluted high-resolution O 1s spectrum. A broad peak is observed at 528.72 eV. This peak corresponds to the binding energy of O2− in the lattice at the O 1s level. The deconvolution of O 1s spectra exhibits two discernible peaks at 527.42 and 529.9 eV, signifying the presence of lattice oxygen and surface adsorbed oxygen, respectively, in the metal oxides (Yuan et al., 2010) The P 2p spectra (Fig. S2h) has a single peak at 131.25 eV, which is assigned to the P–O coordination in PO4 3− (Sahoo et al., 2022). The core-level C 1s, N 1s, and V 2p spectra are presented in Figs. S2e, f, and i, respectively. The C 1s spectra displays two major peaks at 282.3 and 286.3 eV (Fig. S2e). The peak observed at 282.3 eV in the lower C 1s region is associated with graphitic carbons (CC), while the peak at 286.3 eV is linked to the sp2 hybridized carbons found in the –C (-N)2- and –N (-C)3- moieties (Wen et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2013). In Fig. S2f, the N 1s peaks are observed at 397.35 and 396.62 eV. The peaks observed in the spectrum were attributed to the sp2 nitrogen atoms present in the triazine rings (C–N–C). Specifically, the peak at 397.35 eV was assigned to N-(C)3 (Cao et al., 2014).

The optical properties of V@g-C3N4, Ce2Sn2O7, Ag3PO4, and Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 nanocomposites were assessed using the UV-DRS spectra and Tauc plots, as depicted in Figs. S3(a–d) and their insets. The basic absorptions of V@g-C3N4, Ce2Sn2O7, Ag3PO4, and the Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 nanocomposite was observed in the wavelength range of 450–650 nm. The utilization of Tauc plots enabled the determination of the semiconductor band gaps for V@g-C3N4 (2.32 eV), Ce2Sn2O7 (2.51 eV), Ag3PO4 (2.71 eV), and Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 (2.48 eV) nanocomposites (Figs. S3a–d (insets)).

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms presented in Fig. S3e were utilized to assess the porosity and surface areas of the V@g-C3N4, Ce2Sn2O7, and Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 nanocomposites. The standard H3 hysteresis loops displayed by these three materials suggested that the hardened paste's interior pores were mostly slit- and panel-shaped (Wang et al., 2012) The surface areas of the V@g-C3N4, Ce2Sn2O7, and Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 composites were 11.59, 23.31, and 10 m2/g, respectively. Following the development of the composite, there was a decrease in the BET surface area, suggesting that the metals became encapsulated on the surface of V@g-C3N4. This reduced the amount of available empty area for N2 gas molecules. The BJH model was employed to determine the average pore diameters of the V@g-C3N4, Ce2Sn2O7, and Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 composites, as shown in Fig. S3f. The calculated values were found to be 117.93 nm, 118.75 nm, and 31.94 nm, respectively. The nanocomposite of Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 exhibits a reduced pore diameter compared to V@g-C3N4 and Ce2Sn2O7. These pores are structured as mesoporous.

Mott-Schottky measurements were conducted at a frequency of 1000 Hz to examine the flat-band potential (Efb) of V@g-C3N4, Ce2Sn2O7, and Ag3PO4. The results are illustrated in Fig. 2a–c. The Efb of V@g-C3N4, Ce2Sn2O7, and Ag3PO4 versus an Ag/AgCl electrode were −1.31, −0.90, and −1.23 eV, respectively. Using the formula ENHE = Ag/AgCl + 0.197, the values of −1.11, −0.70, and −1.03 eV for the CB at NHE were calculated for V@g-C3N4, Ce2Sn2O7, and Ag3PO4, respectively. By utilizing Eq (2), the valence band (VB) values of V@g-C3N4, Ce2Sn2O7, and Ag3PO4 were determined (Ismael and Wark, 2022).
(2)
E CB
=
E VB
+
E g
where the energy value of the band gap is denoted as Eg. Consequently, it can be concluded that V@g-C3N4, Ce2Sn2O7, and Ag3PO4 exhibit valence bands (EVB) with energy values of 1.21, 1.81, and 1.68 eV (Vs NHE), respectively.

The results of the photocurrent response under light irradiation for the V@g-C3N4, Ce2Sn2O7, Ag3PO4, and Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 2d. The photocurrent intensity of all materials displayed consistent stability, with the highest transient photocurrent density observed in Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4. Results indicate that the implementation of the dual-Z-scheme heterojunction design provides a favorable outcome in enhancing the efficiency of separating charge carriers.

EIS technique was used to investigate the electron transfer properties of the electrode interface in a 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution with 0.1 M KCl. As shown in Fig. 2e, the spectra of the bare GCE, V@g-C3N4/GCE, Ce2Sn2O7/GCE, Ag3PO4/GCE, and Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4/GCE were measured, and the Randles equivalent circuit image is displayed in Fig. 2e (inset). According to Nyquist plots, Ce2Sn2O7/GCE and Ag3PO4/GCE exhibited higher Rct values than the bare GCE, indicating repulsion between the redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and metal oxides (Prasanna et al., 2024). In contrast, the Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 ternary nanocomposites showed a much smaller Rct (102.9 Ω) owing to the improvement in the performance of the photocatalysis, which increased the adsorption capability and separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers and the recombination efficiency of the photoinduced electron-hole pairs was reduced (Cheng et al., 2017). Fig. 2f and S4 show the phase and Bode plots, respectively, of the bare GCE, V@g-C3N4/GCE, Ce2Sn2O7/GCE, Ag3PO4/GCE, and Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4/GCE. In this photodegradation study, the lower phase angle and low frequency in the bode plot of Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4/GCE approves the improved transfer rates of the photoinduced charge carriers and separation efficiency than bare GCE, V@g-C3N4/GCE, Ce2Sn2O7/GCE, Ag3PO4/GCE.

The removal of TTZ (20 mg L−1) using the Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 photocatalyst presence of PS and visible-light irradiation was also investigated (Fig. 3 a). To achieve equilibrium between the adsorption and desorption of TTZ, the reaction mixture was agitated for 30 min in the dark without PS prior to the photocatalytic degradation. Consequently, the light is turned on and PS is incorporated after a 30 min period of adsorption in the absence of light for all subsequent experiments. The investigation of comparison between different photoactive systems, including single components and heterojunction, as well as the presence or absence of PS. In contrast, only PS with the assistance of simulated visible light could give 27.85% TTZ removal, confirming that light can activate PS for photodegradation. In the presence of PS, light irradiation helped to increase the removal rates of V@g-C3N4, Ce2Sn2O7, Ag3PO4, and Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 increased to 17.57%, 31.14%, 78.17%, and 93.2%, respectively, suggesting that Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 was an ideal photocatalyst for TTZ degradation and the UV–visible degradation curves of Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 is shown in Fig. S11a. Catalysis conducted using Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 offered excellent degradation due to two variables. First, the incorporation of V@g-C3N4 and Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4 effectively inhibited the recombination of photogenerated carriers. Second, the extra PS may produce more reactive radicals.

Fig. 3b, the removal of TTZ and followed the pseudo-first-order kinetics. Presence of PS, the rate constants of Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 were higher than other photocatalysts. In addition, the dosage of the Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 nanocomposite is an important role in the photodegradation of TTZ. Fig. 3c shows the effects on dosage of Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 (5, 10, 15, and 20 mg L−1). When 10 mg L−1 Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 was added to the degradation system presence of light irradiation (Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4/PS), a degradation efficiency of 85.33% (0.0993 min−1) was achieved within 50 min. The degradation rate showed no significant increase when the Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4/PS dosage was raised to 15 (0.0596 min−1) and 20 mg L−1 (0.0406 min−1). This can be attributed to the lower light penetration observed at higher concentrations. Fig. 3d illustrates the corresponding rate of reaction. When employing Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4/PS, the concentration of 10 mg L−1 is determined to be the optimal choice for TTZ degradation.

Additionally, the influence of the PS dosage (2–10 mg L−1) on the degradation of TTZ via photodegradation was investigated (Fig. 3e). The TTZ removal efficiency showed an upward trend as the PS dosage increased from 2 to 10 mg L−1. It started at 44% at 2 mg L−1, then increased to 75.22% at 4 mg L−1 and further to 85.91% at 6 mg L−1. However, it slightly decreased to 79.69% at 8 mg L−1 due to the radical quenching effect caused by excessive PS conditions (Yang et al., 2019b). The reaction rates corresponding to different PS concentrations are illustrated in Fig. 3f. Therefore, the most effective PS concentration for the degradation of TTZ is 6 mg L−1.

The degradation rates of TTZ in a Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4/PS/vis system at different TTZ concentrations (10–40 mg L−1) were studied, and the results are shown in Fig. S5a. At low concentrations, TTZ was almost completely degraded within 50 min. In addition, the degradation rate of TTZ decreased with increasing TTZ concentration. This phenomenon can be attributed to the numerous byproducts containing TTZ molecules that consume radicals (Feng et al., 2019) The reaction rates are shown in Fig. S5b. Thus, a TTZ concentration of 20 mg L−1 is appropriate for TTZ degradation.

The impact of pH on the degradation of TTZ in the Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4/PS/vis system was examined at different pH levels (3, 5, 7, 9, and 11), as depicted in Fig. S5c. The degradation rate of TTZ is unfavorable under acidic conditions but reaches almost 93% at pH 7, demonstrating that a neutral environment is more favorable for the degradation of TTZ. This was ascribed to the generation of superoxide's by neutral and alkali-activated PS and production of more hydroxyl radicals via the reaction of PS with hydroxide (Furman et al., 2010). The reaction rates are presented in Fig. S5d. Consequently, a pH of 7 is preferable for TTZ degradation.

Several radical scavengers were employed to detect the radical species produced and establish their relevance in the photodegradation of TTZ. The capture of photoinduced h+ in the catalyst was achieved by utilizing Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) (Xie et al., 2018). Tertiary butyl alcohol (TBA) was employed to capture the ●OH radical, whereas methanol (MeOH) was utilized to capture both the ●OH and SO4 ●− radicals (Yang et al., 2019a). To further eradicate the O2 ●− in the TTZ solution, the utilization of benzoquinone (BQ) was implemented (Zeng et al., 2022). The UV–visible degradation curves of different scavengers as shown Figs. S11b–f. Based on Fig. 4 a, the inclusion of MeOH and TBA resulted in a notable decrease in degradation efficiency from 93.20% to 79.93% and further down to 72.14%. This observation highlights the significance of ●OH and SO4 ●− as crucial active species in the process of degradation. In a similar, the introduction of BQ and EDTA led to a decline in the removal efficiencies of TTZ to 18.53% and 31.14% respectively. This serves as additional evidence highlighting the important roles played by O2 ●− and h+ in the degradation process. Therefore, the reactive oxygen species (ROS) contributions to TTZ removal are in the order of ●OH > SO4 ●− > O2 ●− > h+. In Fig. 4b, the rate constants of TTZ degradation are shown for different radical scavengers. Furthermore, the elimination rate of total organic carbon (TOC) plays a crucial role in assessing the mineralization capacity of catalysts in degrading pollutants. The TOC removal rates and TTZ degradation rates of different catalysts, as depicted in Fig. S12a, exhibited slight inconsistencies due to the conversion of certain TTZ into intermediate products during the reaction. The Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 demonstrated the highest mineralization efficiency, leading to a 70.98% removal of TOC within 50 min.

A series of degradation trials were conducted to assess the reusability of the Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 catalyst. The photocatalyst used in these trials was recovered from previous experiments. After four runs, the degradation removal percentage displayed a notable decrease from 83.99% to 75.22%, as illustrated in Fig. 4c. This outcome underscores the photocatalyst's commendable stability and effectiveness. Fig. 4d displays the respective reaction rates for the four runs. After multiple trials, the Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 composite experienced an unavoidable reduction in mass, resulting in a decline in its degradation efficiency. The findings of this investigation provide strong evidence for the superior reusability and stability of Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 in the degradation of TTZ.

The novel Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 heterojunction exhibits photocatalytic activity, which can be attributed to the presence of TTZ (Fig. 5 ) in the photocatalytic reaction. Furthermore, this photocatalyst is commonly known as a dual Z-scheme heterojunction due to the electron in the VB of Ag3PO4 merging with the holes in the VB of V@g-C3N4. The electrons in the CB of Ag3PO4 and the VB of Ce2Sn2O7 will undergo a similar process of combining with the holes. Consequently, there is an emergence of extra electrons and holes at the CB of Ag3PO4 and the VB of Ce2Sn2O7, respectively. The shifting of electrons and holes in the materials is thermodynamically favorable due to the process driven by the attraction and recombination of opposite charges. Furthermore, the transfer of vacancies from V@g-C3N4 and Ag3PO4 to Ce2Sn2O7 VB occurs because of its higher positive potentials (1.81 eV vs. NHE). Subsequently, these vacancies engage with H2O molecules, leading to the generation of •OH species that effectively degrade TTZ. The electrons generated by light are transferred to the Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4, which activates the PS adsorbed on the surface of the material. This activation leads to the production of •SO4−, which then reacts with H2O to generate •OH (with an energy level of 1.21 eV vs. NHE). Subsequently, the photoexcited electrons in the CB of Ce2Sn2O7 and Ag3PO4 migrate towards the conduction band of V@g-C3N4, driven by its comparatively lower CB potential (−1.11 eV vs. NHE). Upon reaching V@g-C3N4, these electrons engage with dissolved oxygen, resulting in the generation of superoxide radicals (•O2−). The photodegradation of TTZ involves the active involvement of •O2− species.

The supplementary information (Section S4) contains the theoretical information and expressions related to the kinetics studies. All the parameters determined for the three kinetic models are listed in Table S1.
In the Supplementary Information (Section S5), a comprehensive theoretical account of intermediates and potential pathways is presented. Fig. S6 shows the three potential photodegradation pathways.

Using the DFT, the B3LYP/6311++ G (d,p) source of the Gaussian 09 program was utilized to study the physical and chemical behavior of the TTZ molecules (Mahboobi et al., 2020). Fig. S8a shows the chemical structure of TTZ. Fig. S8b depicts the optimized TTZ structure. The HOMO and LUMO diagrams shown in Fig. S8c which demonstrates the area that can easily lose or acquire electrons from the striking radicals. The density of states plots for the TTZ molecules is shown in Fig. S12b. The primary molecule's energy gap (ΔE) is 3.07 eV, and it significantly affects both the molecule's stability and reactivity. Furthermore, calculations were performed to determine the Fukui functions corresponding to nucleophilic f+, electrophilic f−, and radical attack f0. These results are presented in Fig. S12c and Table S2.

The phytotoxicity of TTZ was assessed using mung bean sprouts both before and after degradation. To evaluate the biological toxicity of the final reaction solution, a cultivation study was carried out using Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4/PS/vis treated TTZ solution on mung bean sprouts (H. H. Sun et al., 2021). The mung bean seeds were soaked in ultrapure water, TTZ solution, and Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4/PS/vis-treated TTZ solution overnight at room temperature before being transferred to petri dishes and left to grow for 2 days. After being treated with ultrapure water (Fig. S10a), TTZ solution (Fig. S10b), and the treated TTZ solution (Fig. S10c), the average root lengths were approximately 1.54, 0.71, and 1.44 cm, respectively. The bean sprouts exhibited superior growth in the Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4/PS/vis treated TTZ solution compared to the untreated TTZ solution, although their growth was slightly inferior to that observed in ultrapure water. The results indicated a notable decrease in phytotoxicity in the TTZ solution treated with Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4/PS/vis, as compared to the untreated TTZ solution. The choice of mung bean sprouts in this study was based on their convenience and ease of growth observations, making them an ideal model organism for toxicity assessment. To gain a comprehensive understanding of the risks posed by TTZ and its degradation products, it is strongly recommended to undertake an extensive environmental risk assessment.

In summary, a novel dual z-scheme Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 heterojunction was developed and utilized to PS activation for TTZ photodegradation. The Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4/PS/vis demonstrated a significant TTZ degradation efficiency at 93.20% (k1 = 0.1273 min−1), which is higher than V@g-C3N4/PS/vis, Ce2Sn2O7/PS/vis, and Ag3PO4/PS/vis. The radical trapping test results revealed that four forms of ROS were produced during TTZ degradation which is followed the order, ●OH > SO4 ●− > O2 ●− > h+. Additionally, the TTZ removal efficiency remained between 83.99 and 75.22% after four runs, indicating that Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4 had good recyclability. The phytotoxicity of TTZ was assessed using mung bean sprouts both before and after degradation. The results indicated a notable decrease in phytotoxicity in the TTZ solution treated with Ce2Sn2O7/Ag3PO4/V@g-C3N4/PS/vis, as compared to the untreated TTZ solution. Consequently, this study provides a promising method for removing TTZ from wastewater.
